content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 10 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 207–208 | **Source Images:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/DEU_010_page_001.jpg, DEU_010_page_002.jpg | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), p. 150

---

1 At that time the LORD said to me, "Hew for yourself two tablets of stone [luchot] like the first ones, and come up to me onto the mountain, and make for yourself an ark of wood.

2 And I will write upon the tablets the words that were on the first tablets which you smashed, and you shall put them into the ark."

3 And I made an ark of acacia wood, and I hewed two tablets of stone like the first ones, and I went up onto the mountain with the two tablets in my hand.

4 And he wrote upon the tablets, according to the first writing, the Ten Words [Aseret ha-Devarim] which the LORD spoke to you on the mountain from the midst of the fire on the day of the assembly, and the LORD gave them to me.

5 And I turned and came down from the mountain, and I put the tablets in the ark which I had made, and there they are, as the LORD commanded me.

6 And the sons of Yisra'el journeyed from Be'erot-Benei-Ya'aqan to Moserah; there Aharon died and there he was buried, and Eleazar his son ministered as priest in his place.

7 From there they journeyed to Gudgodah, and from Gudgodah to Yotvath — a land of wadis of water.

8 At that time the LORD set apart the tribe of Levi to carry the ark of the covenant of the LORD, to stand before the LORD to minister to him and to bless in his name, unto this day.

9 Therefore Levi has no portion nor inheritance with his brothers; the LORD is his inheritance, as the LORD your God spoke to him.

10 And I — I stayed on the mountain as the first days, forty days and forty nights, and the LORD listened to me also that time; the LORD was not willing to destroy you.

11 And the LORD said to me, "Arise, go for the journey before the people, and let them go in and possess the land which I swore to their fathers to give to them."

12 And now, Yisra'el, what does the LORD your God ask of you, but to fear the LORD your God, to walk in all his ways and to love him, and to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul,

13 to keep the commandments of the LORD and his statutes which I am commanding you today, for your good?

14 Behold, to the LORD your God belong the heavens and the heaven of heavens, the earth and all that is in it.

15 Only in your fathers did the LORD take delight to love them, and he chose their seed after them — you — above all the peoples, as [it is] this day.

16 And you shall circumcise the foreskin of your heart [mul et orlat levavkhem], and your neck you shall stiffen no more.

17 For the LORD your God, he is the God of gods [Elohei ha-elohim] and the Lord of lords, the great God, the mighty and the awesome, who does not lift up faces and does not take bribes —

18 executing justice for the orphan and the widow, and loving the sojourner, giving him bread and garment.

19 And you shall love the sojourner, for you were sojourners in the land of Egypt.

20 The LORD your God you shall fear; him you shall serve; and to him you shall cling; and by his name you shall swear.

21 He is your praise [tehillatekha] and he is your God who did for you these great and awesome things which your eyes saw.

22 With seventy persons your fathers went down to Egypt, and now the LORD your God has made you as the stars of the heavens in multitude.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**V.1 — luchot:** Hebrew לֻחֹת — "tablets" (of stone). The singular is לוּחַ (luach). The command to "hew" (פְּסָל, pasal) the tablets himself, while the LORD will write upon them, is significant: Moshe makes the material substrate, the divine finger writes the content. The LXX renders πλάκας λιθίνας (stone tablets).

**V.4 — Aseret ha-Devarim:** Hebrew עֲשֶׂרֶת הַדְּבָרִים — "the Ten Words/Things." The familiar "Ten Commandments" or "Decalogue" is literally "the ten words." The EMET renders literally: "the Ten Words." The LXX renders τοὺς δέκα λόγους (the ten words/sayings), exactly paralleling the Hebrew. The term "commandments" (mitsvot) is not used here.

**V.6–7 — itinerary list:** The place-names Beʼerot-Benei-Yaʼaqan, Moserah, Gudgodah, and Yotvath appear in a brief itinerary section. This passage (vv.6–7) represents a shift from first-person Moshe narrative to third-person. The LC manuscript (p.207–208) shows these verses without any marginal notation distinguishing them, though commentators have long noted the editorial seam. LXX Vat. p.150 renders the same itinerary in third person.

**V.9 — "the LORD is his inheritance":** Hebrew יְהוָה הוּא נַחֲלָתוֹ — a theological statement of profound weight: YHWH himself, not land, is the inheritance of the Levitical tribe. The LXX renders Κύριος αὐτὸς κλῆρος αὐτοῦ (the Lord himself [is] his lot/inheritance). The Greek κλῆρος here means "lot" (as in lottery) as well as "inheritance portion."

**V.14 — "heaven of heavens":** Hebrew הַשָּׁמַיִם וּשְׁמֵי הַשָּׁמָיִם — "the heavens and the heavens of the heavens." This is the Hebrew superlative construction using the genitive: "the highest heavens." The LXX renders ὁ οὐρανὸς καὶ ὁ οὐρανὸς τοῦ οὐρανοῦ (the heaven and the heaven of heaven). Rendered literally with "heaven of heavens."

**V.16 — mul et orlat levavkhem:** Hebrew וּמַלְתֶּם אֵת עָרְלַת לְבַבְכֶם — "and you shall circumcise the foreskin of your heart." The verb מוּל (mul) means to circumcise; עָרְלָה (orlah) means foreskin. The metaphorical extension of circumcision to the heart is a major theological move in Deuteronomy (cf. 30:6). The LXX renders περιτμηθήσεσθε τὴν σκληροκαρδίαν ὑμῶν (you shall circumcise the hardness of your heart) — replacing "foreskin" with "hardness," a significant interpretive shift. The MT reading is preserved literally in EMET.

**V.17 — Elohei ha-elohim:** Hebrew אֱלֹהֵי הָאֱלֹהִים וַאֲדֹנֵי הָאֲדֹנִים — "God of gods and Lord of lords." The LXX renders ὁ θεὸς τῶν θεῶν καὶ κύριος τῶν κυρίων (the God of gods and Lord of lords). This formulation does not imply polytheism but rather YHWH's absolute supremacy over all that is called "god."

**V.21 — tehillatekha:** Hebrew תְהִלָּתְךָ — "your praise" or "your boast." Syntactically the verse reads: "He is your praise and he is your God." The LXX renders αὐτὸς καύχημά σου (he [is] your boasting). The MT is striking: the LORD is what Yisra'el boasts in/praises.

— EMET
